title: Mazars Comment - Three Predictions for 2023 - and what they mean for your portfolio
section_path: Mazars Comment - Three Predictions for 2023 - and what they mean for your portfolio
section_path_text: Mazars Comment - Three Predictions for 2023 - and what they mean for your portfolio
summary:
Main
By George Lagarias
The world is never easy to predict. In late 2018, no one really predicted that the world would slow down and the Fed would begin money printing in eight months. In late 2019 no one saw Covid-19 as a serious threat, even if news from China was already worrying.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
By George Lagarias
The world is never easy to predict. In late 2018, no one really predicted that the world would slow down and the Fed would begin money printing in eight months.

children.title: The economics
children.summary: Macroeconomic and market volatility are set to persist throughout 2023. This is not simply an extension of current conditions into the next year.

children.title: The politics
children.summary: The post-WWII World Order is an ‘order' no more. The global landscape is shifting from cooperation to direct competition, even as internal political environments turn more acerbic by the day.

children.title: The conversations we should -and might yet- have
section_path: The conversations we should -and might yet- have
children.section_path_text: The conversations we should -and might yet- have
children.summary: If this writer could ask Santa for one gift, it would be the return of normal conversation. Forums, where all opinions could be discussed in the open without ideological criticism and the dangers of a social media lynching.

children.title: Brexit
children.summary: I am thus somewhat positively surprised that Brexit begins to come more into the conversation, as Britain sees itself falling behind other developed nations in terms of growth while fighting a tight jobs market and possibly lingering inflation. To discuss backtracking would probably be a mistake.

children.title: Global debt
children.summary: The strong Dollar, higher rates and inflation have somewhat curbed global debt issuance. However, at $290bn and 355% Total-Debt-to-GDP, a rising rate environment is a ticking time bomb.

children.title: Central Banks
children.summary: Small groups of unelected officials with the power to create and destroy wealth, accelerate and crush growth should be under constant scrutiny. The time is coming soon to discuss their ‘independent' mandates, how realistic their present inflation targets are, the limits to their decision making and the grounds for international cooperation.

children.title: EU - US - still good?
children.summary: It was French warships which ensured American Independence. And when American soldiers set foot in Normandy, they repaid their debt.
